column1: What is Pivoting (T1090)?
column2: A tactic used by adversaries to proxy traffic in or out of a network when it would normally be disallowed, using a Beacon as the pivot point

column1: What are the two types of pivoting covered in this section?
column2: SOCKS Proxies and Reverse Port Forwards

column1: What does SOCKS stand for?
column2: SOCKet Secure

column1: What is SOCKS?
column2: A protocol that exchanges network packets between a client and server via a proxy server

column1: How does a SOCKS proxy work in a Cobalt Strike context at a high level?
column2: The team server listens on a port, traffic from the adversary's tools is routed through that port, packaged as a task, fetched by Beacon over C2, then forwarded to the internal target host — with responses travelling back the same way

column1: How does Beacon's sleep time affect SOCKS tunnelling speed?
column2: The lower the sleep time, the quicker traffic can be tunnelled through the C2 channel

column1: What are three advantages of tunnelling tools through a SOCKS proxy rather than running them directly on a compromised host?
column2: Some tools are written in languages not available on Windows; it reduces detection surface by removing execution indicators on the host; and it allows GUI tools to be run directly on the attacker machine

column1: What detection indicators are avoided by tunnelling tools remotely via SOCKS instead of running them on the host?
column2: Indicators created by BOF, .NET assembly, or reflective DLL execution that can be logged by security vendors — only network traffic remains

column1: What is Proxifier?
column2: A Windows tool that forces TCP traffic through a proxy server

column1: When configuring a proxification rule in Proxifier, what should be specified to avoid routing all traffic through the proxy?
column2: The IP range and/or domain names of the target internal hosts, so only traffic destined for the internal network goes through the proxy

column1: What are the most common ways to authenticate to domain resources through a SOCKS proxy?
column2: Plaintext credentials or Kerberos tickets (NTLM is considered deprecated in modern tradecraft)

column1: Why does Kerberos authentication through a proxy require static host entries on the attacking machine?
column2: Because Kerberos requires the use of hostnames rather than IP addresses

column1: Can RSAT tools be installed on a non-domain-joined machine?
column2: Yes, the official Remote Server Administration Tools can be installed on any Windows machine regardless of domain join status

column1: How can RSAT PowerShell cmdlets be used with plaintext credentials through a proxy?
column2: By creating a credential object with Get-Credential and passing it to the RSAT cmdlets using the -Credential parameter

column1: When using Kerberos tickets through a proxy, why must service tickets be requested manually?
column2: Because Windows cannot automatically request them in this context — you cannot rely on Windows doing it automatically

column1: What ticket format does Impacket and other Linux tools require for Kerberos authentication?
column2: ccache format (as opposed to the kirbi format used by Windows/Rubeus)

column1: What tool can convert Kerberos tickets between kirbi and ccache formats?
column2: ticketConverter.py (from Impacket)

column1: What environment variable must be set for Linux tools like Impacket to use a Kerberos ticket?
column2: KRB5CCNAME, set to the path of the ccache ticket file on disk

column1: What is proxychains?
column2: A Linux tool that forces TCP traffic made by a given application to route through a configured proxy

column1: Does WSL inherit static host entries added in Windows?
column2: Yes, WSL inherits static host entries from Windows, so they do not need to be added separately to /etc/hosts

column1: What nmap scan flags are recommended when scanning through proxychains?
column2: Use -n (no DNS), -sT (TCP connect scan), and -Pn (skip host discovery), as ICMP and UDP do not work through a SOCKS proxy
